findings.title: Boundary value parsed with `split('boundary=')[-1]` can include trailing junk
findings.detail: When splitting by `'boundary='`, using `[-1]` (the last segment) is incorrect; if `boundary=` appears more than once (e.g. `boundary=foo; boundary=bar`) this returns the last value, not the first. More concretely the issue is subtle: for the test case `"multipart/form-data; boundary=+++ ;"`, the parsed value becomes `b'+++ '` (with a trailing space) because `.split("boundary=")[-1]` returns `"+++ "` (the whole remainder). The code only strips the `section` before the `boundary=` prefix check but NOT the final extracted value. The actual boundary bytes written into the stream will be `+++ ` (with trailing space), which won't match `+++` when parsed. The test `pdict` uses `b"+++"` to verify, so the test would fail or pass by luck depending on cgi's tolerance. Use `.strip()` on the extracted boundary value, not the section.

findings.title: Header lookup uses hardcoded lowercase key but `Headers` object is case-insensitive
findings.detail: The code does `headers["content-type"]` (lowercase) against the `self.headers` (`Headers`) object passed from `_models.py`. The `Headers` class does case-insensitive key lookups via its `__getitem__`, so a check `"content-type" in headers` will work correctly. However, the code only checks `"content-type"` (lowercase). If the caller passes `"Content-Type"` (title-case), the `in` operator works because `Headers.__contains__` lowercases before comparing, but `headers["content-type"]` will also work via `__getitem__`. So for `Headers` objects this is fine. BUT `encode_request` and `encode_multipart_data` accept a plain `Mapping[str, str]` (not necessarily a `Headers` instance), and if a plain dict with `"Content-Type"` (non-lowercase) is passed directly, `"content-type" in headers` would be `False` and the boundary would not be extracted. In practice the call site always passes `self.headers` (a `Headers` object), but the type annotation `Mapping[str, str]` is broader and misleading.

findings.title: Content-Type validation runs even when `content-type` header is not `multipart/form-data` — missing validation for uppercase headers key
findings.detail: The check `if headers and boundary is None and "content-type" in headers` only triggers for lowercase `"content-type"`. Since the `Headers` class normalises keys to lowercase internally and the `in` operator on it lowercases too, this works when the actual object is `Headers`. However the explicit value retrieval `headers["content-type"]` via `Headers.__getitem__` uses a lowercased lookup as well, so there is no bug for `Headers`. But the code silently skips validation when the dict passed uses a different case. The mismatch between documented behaviour and type signature may lead to future misuse.

findings.title: Content-Type header supplied by user is overwritten by `_prepare` in the non-boundary case
findings.detail: When a user supplies a `Content-Type: multipart/form-data; boundary=...` header, `encode_multipart_data` returns `new_headers` which includes a freshly generated (or boundary-respecting) `Content-Type`. This is then passed to `_prepare`, which calls `self.headers.setdefault(key, value)`. Because the user already set `Content-Type` before `_prepare` is called (it's in `self.headers`), the `setdefault` call correctly does NOT overwrite it. So the user's original `Content-Type` is preserved — this works. However, the function `encode_multipart_data` still computes `multipart.get_headers()` which includes a `Content-Type` that duplicates what's already in `self.headers`. The returned `new_headers` Content-Type is redundant but harmless. This is subtle and should be documented.

findings.title: Missing test for invalid content-type (non-multipart) when files are provided
findings.detail: There is no test covering the `ValueError` branch for `content-type` headers that don't start with `multipart/form-data` (e.g. `application/json; boundary=foo`). The error message in that branch is distinct from the 'Missing boundary' message and should have its own test case.

findings.title: `split('boundary=')[-1]` is fragile — should use `split('=', 1)[1]` or partition
findings.detail: Using `section.strip().split('boundary=')[-1]` to extract the boundary value is error-prone: if the boundary value itself contains the string `'boundary='` (contrived but spec-allowed), the wrong suffix is returned. A cleaner approach is `section.strip().split('=', 1)[1].strip()` or `.partition('=')[2].strip()`.

findings.title: Non-multipart `content-type` header raises ValueError instead of being silently ignored
findings.detail: If a user sets a `Content-Type` header to e.g. `application/json` and also passes `files=`, the code raises `ValueError: Invalid content-type header for multipart request`. This is a breaking change in error mode for what was previously a valid (if semantically weird) request — the old code would override the content-type with `multipart/form-data`. The PR should document this new validation behaviour or consider whether it should warn rather than raise.

findings.title: Test case `"multipart/form-data; boundary=+++ ;"` likely tests wrong expected boundary
findings.detail: The test parameterises with `"multipart/form-data; boundary=+++ ;"`. The parser will extract the section `" boundary=+++ "` (after the trailing `;`), strip it to `"boundary=+++ "`, then call `.split('boundary=')[-1]` yielding `"+++ "`. This is then `.encode('latin-1')` giving `b'+++ '` (with trailing space). The test then verifies `pdict = {"boundary": b"+++'}`  (no trailing space), which mismatches. The actual generated stream boundary will contain the trailing space, causing a parse mismatch. This test case may either fail or reveal that trailing space handling needs to be stripped.

findings.title: `MultipartStream.content_type` uses raw boundary bytes decoded as ASCII — may fail for non-ASCII boundaries
findings.detail: When a user supplies a boundary via the `Content-Type` header, the boundary is extracted and encoded as `latin-1` (line 29 of `_multipart.py`), but in `MultipartStream.__init__` (line 195), `boundary.decode('ascii')` is used to construct the stored `content_type`. If the user's boundary contains non-ASCII latin-1 characters, this will raise a `UnicodeDecodeError`. This is an existing issue but the new code path makes it reachable from user input without an obvious error message.
